moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the queen's pawn, establishing central control and preparing to develop our pieces harmoniously. This move immediately stakes a claim to the e5 square and opens diagonal lines for our dark-squared bishop on c1 and our queen. The d4 move is more positional than 1.e4, often leading to slower, strategic games where we gradually build up pressure in the center and on the wings. Our plan typically involves developing the knight to f3, supporting the center with c3 or c4, and completing kingside development before launching our middlegame plans.

Black chooses the rare and provocative Mikenas Defense, immediately challenging our central pawn from an unusual angle while avoiding the more common responses like 1...d5 or 1...Nf6. This knight move puts immediate pressure on our d4 pawn and prepares to support a potential ...e5 advance, but it also blocks the c-pawn and makes it harder for Black to achieve the typical ...d5 break. The Mikenas Defense is a hypermodern opening that aims to control the center from afar rather than occupying it directly, often leading to sharp tactical games where Black seeks quick piece activity and kingside attacking chances. We should respond by reinforcing our center and continuing our development, keeping in mind that Black's knight on c6 can become a target if we play c4-c5 later in the game.
